content.text:
Simplify the module at src/report.ts without changing its behaviour or its public
interface.

Work in 3 iterations. In each iteration, produce three candidate
simplifications of the current code, each pushing in one direction:
"make it more direct", "make it more obvious", "make it smaller".
Then pick the candidate that most improves the code, or merge two if they
compose cleanly — simpler, clearer, behaviour unchanged — and carry it into
the next iteration. Keep a copy of
each iteration's chosen version.

When all 3 iterations are done, compare the kept versions with a
fresh perspective and pick the best one overall: the simplest version that is
still clear, complete, and behaviour-preserving.
The winner need not be the last — simplification can overshoot.

Write the winning version back to src/report.ts and confirm the module's test suite still passes: npx tsx --test <the .test.ts file next to the module>.

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- ask-stakeholder: Fork or resume a saved "stakeholder" session to ask it questions — use to consult a converged plan or persona, or to poll a panel of stakeholders for reactions to a proposal.
- detached-git-init: Initialise a git repo whose git dir lives outside the working tree, under ~/.git-detached/<project>. Use when a project must not contain a .git folder (e.g. iCloud/Obsidian synced dirs).
- reviewables: How to write a reviewable post that distills plans, changesets and proposals for easy consumption by the user.
- vendored-repos: Read-only store of vendored external library source at ~/Repos/vendors — consult it for idiomatic examples, real API usage, and internals before falling back to web search or guessing. Also covers adding a repo to the store.
- vite-plus: When using vite+ (vite plus, vp), use this skill to understand the recommend best practices.
- writing-style: Write in Daniel's voice. Use whenever drafting prose on his behalf — READMEs, blog posts, editorials, PR/issue comments, legal or formal documents.
- create-stakeholder: Save THIS session as a "stakeholder session", and adopt the stakeholder role
- simplify-goal: Simplify a module by iterating, diverging, and selecting
- stakeholders: List the stakeholder sessions saved for the current project

addedBlocks:
## context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service — even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer — your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

content.text:
I've reviewed the module — it's straightforward output wrapped in a lot of unused-generality machinery (a theme provider with one theme, a message catalogue with one locale, an engine with hooks nobody sets, empty extension hooks on the base renderer). I'll run the 3-iteration process, then write the winner back.

**Iteration 1** — direct/obvious/smaller candidates all converge on the same first cut: drop the unused `EngineHooks`/`beforeRender`/`afterRender` hooks (dead extension points), collapse `formatHours` duplication. Kept version: same class shape, hooks removed, one shared `formatHours`.

**Iteration 2** — candidates: (a) direct: replace `ThemeProvider`/`MessageCatalogue`/`RendererRegistry`/`RendererFactory` classes with plain lookup objects since each only ever holds one entry; (b) obvious: turn `SectionRenderer` classes into named functions in a `Record<string, fn>`; (c) smaller: merge summary/footer duplicate counting loops. Chosen: merge (a)+(b) — plain objects/functions is both more direct and more obvious, and eliminates ~150 lines of class ceremony.

**Iteration 3** — with everything now data + functions, candidates are mostly about local shape (arrow vs function, inlining single-use helpers). Chosen: single-pass counting for tasks (done/open/hours computed once, shared by summary and footer) — smaller and removes a duplicate loop.

Writing the winner (iteration 2+3 merged; overshooting further into one giant function would hurt clarity, so I stopped here).

lines: +  /** Only plain text output is implemented; the enum anticipates markdown. */
- * A theme bundles the characters used to draw the report. Only the default
- * theme ships today; the provider exists so branded reports can be added
-  private readonly themes = new Map<string, Theme>();
-      throw new Error(`no theme registered under: ${name}`);
- * Localisable message fragments. Only "en" is provided; the catalogue exists
- * so translated reports can be introduced without touching the renderers.
-      throw new Error(`no messages for locale: ${this.locale}`);
-      throw new Error(`no message for key: ${key}`);
+    throw new Error(`no theme registered under: ${name}`);
+    throw new Error(`no messages for locale: ${locale}`);
+function taskTotals(tasks: Task[]): { doneCount: number; openCount: number; totalHours: number } {
+  return { doneCount, openCount: tasks.length - doneCount, totalHours };
-  protected afterRender(_context: RenderContext, lines: string[]): string[] {
-  private readonly renderers = new Map<string, SectionRenderer>();
-      throw new Error(`no renderer for section: ${section}`);
-  /** Only plain text output is implemented; the enum anticipates markdown. */
+    throw new Error(`no renderer for section: ${section}`);
export function formatReport(input: ReportInput, options?: ReportOptions): string {

content.text: All 9 tests pass. `src/report.ts` went from 355 lines of class ceremony (theme provider, message catalogue, line buffer, renderer registry/factory, engine with unused hooks) down to ~130 lines of plain data + functions, same public interface (`Task`, `ReportInput`, `ReportOptions`, `formatReport`), identical behaviour.
